reasoning: 2023 and 2024 were shaped by a strong El Niño event, driving record-breaking global temperatures. The year following the peak and decay of a strong El Niño (e.g., 2025) typically transitions into an ENSO-neutral or La Niña phase, meaning global average temperatures usually drop relative to the preceding peak years (as seen in 1999 following 1998, or 2017 following 2016). Given this cyclical nature imposed over the secular warming trend, 2025 is highly unlikely to beat the expected records set in 2023 and 2024. I estimate a 10% chance due to background warming acceleration and the possibility of anomalous non-ENSO forcings.
